---

Psychographic Research: Understanding
Your Market's Psychology
Welcome to this comprehensive guide on psychographic research, a critical component of effective market research. While
demographic research focuses on basic facts about your audience (age, income, location), psychographic research delves
deeper into their psychology4their hopes, fears, beliefs, and attitudes. This document will walk you through the importance of
psychographic research, key questions to explore, how to implement it effectively, and how to use the insights to create more
compelling marketing messages.
As we explore this topic, we'll see how understanding your market on a psychological level allows you to connect with them
more authentically, address their true pain points, and position your product as the ideal solution. We'll also discuss how to
balance the depth of research with the need for speed in today's AI-powered marketing landscape.

-- 1 of 8 --

The Value of Psychographic Research
Psychographic research goes beyond traditional demographic data to understand the psychology of your target market. While
demographics tell you
who your customers are, psychographics tell you
why they make decisions and what truly motivates
them.
Deeper Understanding
Psychographic research reveals the
underlying motivations, fears, and
aspirations that drive purchasing
decisions, allowing you to connect
with your audience on a more
meaningful level.
Better Messaging
When you understand your
market's psychology, you can craft
messages that resonate with their
worldview, addressing their specific
concerns and speaking their
language.
Competitive Edge
While competitors may have similar
demographic data, deep
psychographic insights give you a
unique advantage in positioning
your product and connecting
emotionally with your audience.
In today's fast-paced marketing environment, there's a temptation to skim through research or rely solely on AI-generated
summaries. While these tools are valuable for efficiency, it's worth taking the time4especially when you're new to a market4
to thoroughly read through psychographic research. This initial investment in understanding your audience will pay dividends
in more effective marketing messages.
As the presenter emphasizes: "You cannot move slow anymore" in today's marketing landscape, but that doesn't mean
sacrificing depth of understanding. The key is finding the balance between speed and comprehension, using AI tools to
accelerate the process while still internalizing the critical insights about your market's psychology.

-- 2 of 8 --

Key Psychographic Questions to Explore
Effective psychographic research involves asking specific questions that reveal the deeper psychological aspects of your target
market. These questions help you understand not just what your market wants, but why they want it and what barriers they
perceive.
Basic Demographics
While not strictly psychographic, understanding who your
customer is provides context for their psychological profile:
Age range (e.g., 45+ women, 18-35 men)
Income level and occupation
Geographic location (urban, rural, etc.)
Family status (married, single, with children)
Attitudes and Beliefs
Understanding their worldview helps you align your
messaging with their existing beliefs:
Political leanings (conservative, liberal, etc.)
Religious or spiritual beliefs
Views on wealth and success
Attitudes toward authority and institutions
Environmental or social concerns
Hopes and Dreams
What do they aspire to achieve
related to the pain point your
product addresses? For
example, someone interested in
skincare might hope to "feel
confident and sexy in their own
skin" or "love who they see
looking back in the mirror"4
going beyond just "removing
wrinkles."
Victories and Failures
How have they tried to solve
their pain point in the past?
What worked (victories) and
what didn't (failures)? For
instance, someone seeking a
side business might consider it a
victory that they have financial
stability from their main job, but
a failure that previous side
hustles wasted time and money.
Perceived External
Barriers
What outside forces do they
believe have prevented their
success? This could include "the
system," "big pharma,"
"environmental toxins," or "the
economy." Understanding these
perceived barriers helps you
acknowledge their worldview
and position your product as a
solution that works despite
these obstacles.
These questions form the foundation of psychographic research and were pioneered in the RMBC (Research, Mechanism,
Brief, Copy) framework. By systematically exploring these areas, you develop a comprehensive understanding of your market's
psychology that informs more effective marketing messages.

-- 3 of 8 --

Understanding Prejudices and Core Beliefs
Exploring your market's prejudices and core beliefs provides crucial insights that can dramatically improve your marketing
effectiveness. These aren't necessarily negative traits, but rather biases and fundamental worldviews that influence how your
audience perceives products and messages.
Market Prejudices
In this context, "prejudices" refers to biases or preconceived notions your market holds4not necessarily racial or
discriminatory attitudes. These biases often relate directly to your product category or competing solutions:
Institutional Biases
Many markets have strong biases against institutions like "big pharma," "big government," or "big business."
Understanding these allows you to position your product as an alternative to the "establishment" solutions they
distrust.
Age-Related Biases
For example, women 45+ seeking skincare solutions may have prejudices against younger beauty counter
representatives who "don't understand" their concerns. Similarly, younger people seeking business opportunities
might have biases against baby boomers who "got lucky" with easier economic conditions.
Product Biases
Your market may have strong opinions about certain ingredients, approaches, or types of products. They might
believe natural products are always better than synthetic ones, or that traditional methods are superior to modern
innovations.
Core Beliefs About Life, Love, and Family
Understanding your market's fundamental beliefs about what matters in life helps you connect with their values and
aspirations:
What do they believe constitutes a good life?
How do they define success and happiness?
What role does family play in their decision-making?
What values do they hold most dear?
What legacy do they hope to leave?
These insights allow you to craft marketing messages that acknowledge and respect your audience's worldview. When you
demonstrate that you understand their biases and share their values, you build trust and credibility that makes your marketing
more persuasive. As the presenter notes, these insights can dramatically change how you position your product and the
language you use to describe its benefits.

-- 4 of 8 --

Analyzing Existing Solutions
A comprehensive psychographic analysis must include an examination of how your market currently addresses their pain
points. Understanding their experience with existing solutions provides valuable insights into their expectations, frustrations,
and what they truly value in a product or service.
Mapping the Solution Landscape
For any pain point, multiple solution categories typically exist. For example, weight loss might be addressed through
supplements, workout programs, gym memberships, GLP1 medications, online coaching, diets, and more. Understanding this
landscape helps you position your product effectively.
Identify Current Solutions
What products, services, or approaches is your market
already using to address their pain point? Create a
comprehensive list of all potential solution categories.
Evaluate Experiences
Research how your market feels about these solutions.
What do they like? What frustrates them? Have they had
any particularly positive or negative experiences?
Collect Horror Stories
Look for dramatic negative experiences with existing
solutions. These "horror stories" can be powerful in your
marketing to highlight the risks of alternative approaches.
Assess Effectiveness Beliefs
Does your market believe existing solutions work? This is
crucial for positioning4you'll take a different approach if
they're skeptical of all solutions versus if they believe in a
competitor's effectiveness.
Example: Contrasting with GLP1 Medications
The presenter uses GLP1 medications (like Ozempic) as an example. Even though people believe these medications work for
weight loss, there are still aspects they don't like:
High cost
Need for self-injection
Concerns about being dependent on medication
Potential side effects
Understanding these pain points allows you to position an alternative solution that addresses these specific concerns, even
when competing against an effective product. This analysis helps you identify the unique value proposition that will resonate
most with your market based on their actual experiences and concerns with existing solutions.

-- 5 of 8 --

Exploring Curiosity and Corruption Angles
Two powerful psychological angles in marketing are curiosity about forgotten or suppressed solutions, and narratives about
how outside forces have corrupted or worsened the market's pain point. These approaches tap into deep psychological
triggers that can make your marketing more compelling and persuasive.
The Curiosity Angle: Rediscovered Solutions
People are naturally drawn to the idea of rediscovered wisdom or solutions that were once effective but have been forgotten
or suppressed. This creates a powerful narrative for your product.
1	Historical Solution
Identify a historical attempt to solve the problem
that was unique, effective, or interesting. Example:
"During World War II, undecylenic acid was
discovered to combat nail fungus in troops
stationed in damp tropical environments."
2 Suppression or Forgetting
Explain why this solution was lost to history. Was it
suppressed by powerful interests? Simply
forgotten as fashions changed? Example: "Nikola
Tesla's energy innovations were discredited and
thrown into the ash heap of history."
3	Rediscovery
Position your product as rediscovering or
improving upon this forgotten wisdom. Example:
"The dust bowl farmer wives of 1930s Oklahoma
who had perfect glowing skin because they knew
about this remedy we're now bringing back."
The Corruption Angle: Paradise Lost
This narrative suggests that the market's pain point used to be less severe or non-existent until corrupting forces made things
worse. This taps into nostalgia and righteous indignation.
"Is there a belief that the market's pain point used to not exist, or used to not be so bad? Is there a belief that it has been
recently exacerbated by outside forces? If so, what are those forces, and what's the reason behind the present situation?"
Examples of Corruption Narratives:
Food industry corruption: "In the 1980s, tobacco scientists were hired by food companies to make products more
addictive, adding chemicals and preservatives that have worsened our health."
Dietary misinformation: "Dr. Ancel Keys' promotion of low-fat diets in the 1950s led to increased carbohydrate
consumption and processed foods, causing the rise in obesity and diabetes."
Ancient health wisdom: "Why do fossilized teeth from 2,000 years ago show no cavities despite no dental care, while
modern teeth decay despite advanced dentistry?"
These narratives are powerful because they suggest your product isn't just a solution4it's a return to a better way that was
lost or taken from us. They position your customer not just as a consumer, but as someone reclaiming something that should
have been theirs all along.

-- 6 of 8 --

Implementing Psychographic Research
with AI
Modern AI tools have revolutionized how we conduct psychographic research, making it faster and more comprehensive than
ever before. However, balancing speed with depth of understanding remains crucial for effective marketing.
The Speed vs. Depth Balance
The presenter emphasizes an important point about research in the age of AI: "You cannot move slow anymore." With
powerful AI research tools at our disposal, there's no excuse for prolonged research phases. However, he also cautions against
skimming too superficially, especially when you're new to a market:
"Especially if you're new to a market or you don't really have a ton of experience, it's worthwhile to just read through
everything at least the first time... There will always be little insights that might end up being really valuable that might get
missed when you summarize stuff with LLMs."
The Research Process
Create a Comprehensive Prompt
Develop a prompt that includes all the psychographic
questions discussed earlier. Tell the AI you're writing
copy for a specific demographic and need deep research
to answer these questions.
Generate Initial Research
Use an AI tool like Claude or GPT to generate
comprehensive psychographic research based on your
prompt. This provides a foundation of insights about your
market.
Expand Pain Points
If the initial research doesn't provide enough depth on
pain points, run additional prompts specifically focused
on expanding this area.
Combine Multiple Sources
If using multiple AI tools or prompts, combine the insights
into a single comprehensive psychographic document
that can inform all your marketing efforts.
Leveraging the Research
Once you have comprehensive psychographic research, you can:
Feed it to AI tools when writing different types of copy
Use it to identify powerful angles and hooks for ads
Reference it when creating sales letters, VSLs, or product pages
Develop more resonant messaging that speaks directly to your market's psychology
The presenter notes that this approach to psychographic research was "revolutionary" when first introduced in the RMBC
framework, as it went beyond traditional demographic research to understand markets on a deeper level. Today, with AI tools,
we can implement this approach more efficiently than ever while still capturing the nuanced psychological insights that make
marketing truly effective.

-- 7 of 8 --

Applying Psychographic Insights to
Marketing
The ultimate value of psychographic research lies in how you apply these insights to create more effective marketing
messages. Understanding your market's psychology allows you to craft messages that resonate on a deeper level, addressing
their true motivations and speaking to their worldview.
Tailoring Messages to Psychological Profiles
The presenter provides several examples of how psychographic insights can dramatically change your messaging approach:
Economic Attitudes Example
For a business opportunity product, your messaging would
differ significantly based on your market's attitudes toward
wealth:
If they believe wealth is unfairly distributed: "Finally,
you can level the playing field and access the secret that
greedy 1% billionaires have been keeping from the rest
of us."
If they believe in meritocracy: "The same secret that
Elon Musk and Steve Jobs used to become billionaires is
something anyone can use to start a profitable side
hustle if you're willing to put in the work."
Age-Related Prejudices Example
For skincare products targeting women 45+:
Acknowledging their prejudice: "You're tired of 25-
year-old beauty counter reps who don't understand
your skin concerns telling you what to do."
Positioning your solution: "Created by women who
understand the unique challenges of mature skin
because we're living it too."
Leveraging Corruption and Curiosity Narratives
The Corruption Angle
For a natural health product: "Food
wasn't always this harmful. In the
1980s, tobacco scientists were
hired by food companies, adding
chemicals and preservatives that
have destroyed our health. Look at
how rates of obesity, diabetes, and
heart disease have skyrocketed
since then. Our product helps you
reclaim the natural health humans
enjoyed before our food system
was corrupted."
The Curiosity Angle
For a talc-based beauty product:
"The dust bowl farmer wives of
1930s Oklahoma had perfect,
glowing skin despite harsh
conditions. Their secret? A simple
talc remedy that's been forgotten
by modern beauty companies
focused on selling you expensive
chemicals. We've rediscovered this
powerful natural solution and made
it even better."
The External Barrier
Angle
For a health supplement: "You may
feel the deck is stacked against you.
With toxins in our water,
microplastics everywhere, and
pollution in the air, it might seem
hopeless. I felt that way too. But
there's something you can do to
protect your health despite these
challenges..."
The Power of Psychographic Research
By understanding your market's psychology4their hopes, fears, beliefs, and biases4you can create marketing that doesn't just
sell a product but speaks to who they are and what they truly want. This deeper connection is what separates average
marketing from campaigns that generate exceptional results.
As marketing continues to evolve with AI tools making research faster and more comprehensive, the marketers who will
succeed are those who use these tools to gain deeper psychological insights while maintaining the human touch that translates
those insights into authentic, resonant messaging.

-- 8 of 8 --
